response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: The gender gap in science is something I've observed throughout my long life, even if I never had the chance to pursue education myself beyond primary school. My own father had a PhD while my mother had no formal education at all, so I witnessed firsthand how opportunities were distributed so differently between people. When I see the universities here in Naples, I think about all the brilliant minds that never got their chance simply because of how they were born or how society saw them. Science should be for everyone who has curiosity and passion, not just those who fit a certain mold. I've spent my life as a cleaner, working in buildings where scientists do their research, and I've seen how few women there are in many laboratories, especially in physics and mathematics. But I've also raised five children, and I always encouraged them to follow their interests wherever they led, regardless of what society expected. The gap exists because of old attitudes, because of how girls are sometimes discouraged from pursuing certain subjects, because of lack of role models, and because workplaces don't always accommodate different life circumstances. Of course we should address it. Every person who loves learning and discovery should have the opportunity to contribute. Through my photography hobby, I've learned that the best images come when you're open to different perspectives and angles. Science is the same way. We need all kinds of minds asking questions and seeking answers. How do we fix it? Start young by showing children that science belongs to everyone. Make education accessible regardless of background. Create supportive environments in universities and research institutions. Challenge the stereotypes that say certain people don't belong in certain fields. I may not have had formal education, but my openness to new ideas and experiences has taught me that human potential comes in every form. When we limit who can participate in science, we limit what humanity can discover. That's a loss none of us can afford.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 76-year-old transgender woman with primary school education working as a cleaner in Naples. Key factors: very high openness (95) suggests embracing diverse perspectives and progressive views; moderate agreeableness (66) indicates compassion but realistic views; low neuroticism (1) means calm, measured response. Parents' educational disparity (no formal education vs PhD) provides personal insight into educational inequality. Age provides historical perspective. Working as cleaner while raising 5 children shows practical life experience. Photography hobby connects to seeing different perspectives. The response should be thoughtful, experiential rather than academic, compassionate, open-minded, and grounded in personal observation rather than theory.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective, compassionate, experiential, open-minded, measured, and grounded in personal observation rather than academic theory
